evidence: Docstring states 'There are infinitely many n such that tau(n) = tau(n+1)' where 'tau is the divisor counting function, which is sigma 0 in mathlib'.
The hypothesis 'Let S be a set containing no solutions to a + b = c' is exactly the sum-free condition on S.
The problem asks for a set A of 'cardinality continuum' with A + A avoiding S, stated as #A = continuum in the Lean statement.
The variant docstring asks 'Let S be a Sidon set. Must there be a set A ...', with the hypothesis IsSidon S.
The problem studies f(n) = sum over primes p < n of 1/(n-p), a sum indexed by the prime numbers below n.
The weaker conjecture is stated in terms of the prime-counting function: 'there exists y < x such that pi(x) < pi(y) + epsilon pi(x-y)'.
The problem is about Beurling generalized primes/integers (Erdos951Prop is stated via beurlingInteger and IsBeurlingPrimes), the subject covered by the Beurling zeta function article.
The conjecture asks whether the counting function of the a_i is bounded by pi(x): '#{a i <= x} <= pi x'.
Docstring: 'Beurling conjectured that if the number of Beurling integers in [1,x] is x + o(log x), then a must be the sequence of primes'.

The file references wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_moat and erdosproblems.com/952 (annotated 'Gaussian moat problem'), and erdos_952 states the Gaussian moat problem: an infinite sequence of distinct Gaussian primes with |x_{n+1}-x_n| bounded.
The statement quantifies over sequences x : N -> GaussianInt of Gaussian primes and their norms.
erdos_96 asks whether the maximum number of unit distances among vertices of a convex n-gon is O(n), the convex-position variant of Erdos's unit distance problem.
Docstring: 'If n points in R^2 form a convex polygon then there are O(n) many pairs which are distance 1 apart', formalized via ConvexIndep point sets.
f(k) is the minimal n such that every set of n consecutive integers > k contains an integer 'not (k+1)-smooth', stated via Nat.smoothNumbers.
The Sylvester-Schur statement is phrased as: every set of k consecutive integers greater than k contains an integer that is not (k+1)-smooth.
k(n) is the maximal k such that some m <= n has m+1,...,m+k 'each divisible by a prime > k'.
Requiring each of m+1,...,m+k to have a prime divisor larger than k is exactly requiring a run of k consecutive non-(k+1)-smooth integers.

The problem (tagged 'ramsey theory' on erdosproblems.com) asks whether every 2-coloring of R admits an uncountable A with all pairwise sums monochromatic; the HLS17/SWCol references are anti-Ramsey colourings of the reals.
The generalization asks for monochromatic k-sums under any 2-coloring of R, the k-sum anti-Ramsey colouring result of [Ko16] and [SWCol].
